text: Hello friends, I am Dr. Rahul Mahadhar, today going to present laparoscopic nascent fundoplication step by step for hiatal hernia. Forty eight year old female patient presented with the volume reflux, upper GI endoscopy revealed hiatal hernia with grade one GRD. There were no comorbidities in this patient and her BMI was thirty two point five. Manometry study in her revealed hypotensive alias with hiatal hernia and there were normal mobility of the esophagus. So plan in this patient is laparoscopic nascent fundoplication.

The surgical setup is displayed, showing the patient positioned supine with ports placed for laparoscopic access. The surgeon stands between the patient's legs, with two monitors positioned at shoulder level. The anesthesia trolley is located at the head end, and the assistant stands on the patient's left side.
The surgeon uses a Nathanson's retractor to lift the left lobe of the liver, revealing a large hiatal hernia with the stomach protruding into the chest cavity, creating a visible gap.

A 5mm port is placed at the anterior axillary line below the left costal margin. Using two hand instruments, the surgeon performs the past flaccida approach, cutting the past flaccida and the peritoneum over the right crus with a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take small bites of the peritoneum over the right crus, carefully cutting the tissue while achieving hemostasis.

The assistant provides traction, revealing the peritoneal lining over the right crus. The surgeon makes a small incision with a harmonic scalpel, entering the plane while preserving the peritoneal lining over the right crus. White fascia structures are visible, which are dissected laterally.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the right pleura using a harmonic scalpel and a left-hand instrument, ensuring not to injure the pleura. The posterior vagus nerve is visible during the dissection.

The assistant provides traction towards the right side of the patient, revealing the lateral attachments on the left side. The surgeon holds the peritoneum and pushes the structures downward, exposing the peritoneal lining over the left cusp while maintaining the integrity of the peritoneal lining over the Rura on the left side.

The surgeon uses the left hand instrument to grasp the peritoneal lining over the left crus and cuts it with a harmonic scalpel, ensuring the lining is maintained. The anterior vagus nerve is visible during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully pushes down the vagus nerve and enters the plane above it, separating the lower esophagus anteriorly. The anterior vagus is visible and must be preserved to avoid injury. Small vessels in the area are cut using a harmonic scalpel to prevent bleeding, with the aorta visible posteriorly.
The assistant holds the OG junction and lifts it, revealing the posterior vagus and esophagus. The surgeon uses a left-hand instrument to push and dissect the structures, creating a posterior window.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a harmonic scalpel, carefully cutting small vessels to minimize bleeding. The posterior vagus nerve is visible above the dissection area.
The surgeon places umbilical tape around the esophagogastric (OG) junction to facilitate maneuverability, while the assistant holds the tape to provide traction.

The surgeon performs trans-hiatal dissection, lifting the posterior vagus nerve to separate it from the aorta, which is visible posteriorly. Careful identification of structures is necessary to avoid injuring the pleura, with efforts to minimize bleeding in the area.

The surgeon uses small bites with the harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue, revealing the aorta posteriorly. Dissection is carried out around the left crus, where the peritoneal reflection is visible. The peritoneal reflection is pushed to create a plane between the esophagus and the peritoneal reflection.

The surgeon performs a meticulous dissection using small bites, applying traction and counter traction to carefully separate tissues. The peritoneum is maintained over the left crus to facilitate future pleural suturing.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection using a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating the lower esophagus from pleural attachments on the left side to avoid pleural injury. The aorta is visible below the dissection area.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect small fibers between the pericardium and the intraabdominal esophagus, aiming to achieve sufficient esophageal length. Posteriorly, flimsy attachments and small vessels are carefully cut to avoid bleeding.

The surgeon uses umbilical tape marked in one-centimeter increments to measure the mobilized esophagus, showing a length of six centimeters with traction. The intra-abdominal esophagus length is measured at approximately three centimeters below the crura without traction.

The surgeon completes the dissection and begins addressing the short gastric vessels at the lower end of the spleen, dissecting along the greater curvature of the stomach. The assistant holds the cut end while the surgeon uses a ligature to carefully cut along the greater curvature, ensuring the blood supply to the stomach is maintained to prevent ischemic injury.
The surgeon uses a ligature to carefully dissect attachments along the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring hemostasis to prevent bleeding and maintain a clear surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects the short gastric vessels near the spleen, applying lateral traction with the assistant's help. Ligature or bipolar instruments are used initially, followed by harmonic scalpel to manage any adhesions and prevent bleeding in this technically demanding area.

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing both the crura and the upper border of the pancreas. The fundus wrap is passed through the posterior window, and the shoeshine maneuver is performed, showing smooth movement of the tissue.

The surgeon performs crural approximation using 2-0 sutures, carefully monitoring the needle's entry and exit points to avoid injury to the underlying aorta and caudate lobe of the liver. The floppy Nissen fundoplication is visible and remains stable without retracting.
The surgeon places three throws of suture to secure the crural approximation, ensuring the suture remains in place without additional assistance.
The surgeon places an additional stitch with three throws to further approximate the tissue, ensuring secure suture placement.

The surgeon completes coronal suturing, ensuring the approximation is not too tight. The surgical field shows the sutured area with careful attention to maintaining normal motility as suggested by previous manometry results.

The surgeon prepares to perform a Nissen fundoplication by wrapping the fundus around the lower esophagus. The assistant holds the umbilical tape to maintain position. Adhesions between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach are identified, and the surgeon begins to cut these adhesions to free the posterior part of the stomach.

The assistant provides traction while the surgeon uses a ligature to release dense adhesions on the posterior aspect of the stomach. The adhesions are carefully cut to ensure the stomach is free for a floppy Nissen fundoplication. The splenic artery is visible, and care is taken to avoid injury to it during the adhesion separation.

The surgeon ensures the splenic artery is intact and visible, while the assistant holds traction around the esophagogastric junction. The surgeon prepares to create a 360-degree wrap around the junction.
The surgeon observes that the structure is vented and not retracting. Sutures are placed laterally to create a 360-degree wrap around the lower esophagus.
The surgeon performs a floppy Nissen fundoplication by placing a second stitch from the stomach to the esophagus and back to the stomach, securing the wrap just above the esophagogastric junction.
The surgeon places the third suture at the level of the esophagogastric junction, securing the wrap to the right crura to prevent migration into the chest.
The surgeon places the second suture posterior to the wrap, securing it to both crura to prevent migration during the post-operative period.

The surgeon completes the procedure by removing the Nathanson's retractor under visualization, ensuring there is no bleeding. A Ryles tube is inserted by the anesthetist, and the umbilical tape is removed.
